title: Academic honesty and citation
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to academic honesty and citation for the IB Extended Essay. It moves beyond simply avoiding plagiarism, framing citation as a critical tool for building a high-scoring essay. The content is directly aligned with EE assessment criteria B (Argument), C (Engagement), and D (Presentation), explaining how scholarly practice underpins success in each.
learningObjectives: Understand the IB's definition of academic misconduct and its relationship to the Learner Profile attribute 'Principled'.
Master the techniques of quoting, paraphrasing, and summarising, and know when to cite each to support an argument (Criterion B).
Apply a consistent academic citation style (e.g., MLA, APA, Chicago) to in-text citations, footnotes, and the final bibliography.
Evaluate how the breadth and quality of sources demonstrate research engagement for Criterion C.
Recognise the non-negotiable role of formal citation in achieving top marks for Criterion D (Presentation).

simpleExplanation.title: Citation: Your Academic GPS
simpleExplanation.summary: Citation isn't just about rules and avoiding plagiarism; it's about showing your reader the intellectual journey you've taken. Like a GPS map, your citations guide the examiner through your research, proving where you found your evidence and demonstrating how your ideas connect to the wider academic world.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you are a detective presenting your case in court. You cannot simply declare, 'The butler did it.' You must present evidence: witness statements, forensic reports, fingerprints, and a motive. In your EE, your citations are this evidence. Direct quotes are the witness's exact words, paraphrased ideas are your summary of their testimony, and your bibliography is the complete case file. Without this evidence, your argument is just an unsubstantiated theory; with it, it becomes a compelling, credible case.
steps: Track Everything from Day One: Use a reference manager (like Zotero) or a detailed document to record every source you consult. Note the author, title, year, and page numbers for specific ideas or quotes. This is your research log.

Cite as You Write: Do not wait until the end. As you draft each paragraph, insert a placeholder in-text citation, like '(Smith 2021, p. 45)'. This prevents accidental plagiarism and makes the final formatting process much easier.

Choose and Master One Style: Consult with your supervisor, pick one recognised citation style (e.g., MLA, APA, Chicago), and stick with it. Consistency across your in-text citations and bibliography is essential for Criterion D.

Cross-Reference and Perfect: Before submission, check that every in-text citation matches an entry in your bibliography, and that every source in the bibliography is cited in your essay. Meticulously format both according to your chosen style guide.

flashcards.back: Behaviour, whether deliberate or accidental, that gives an unfair advantage to a candidate. It includes plagiarism, collusion, duplication of work, and falsifying data. It is a breach of the IB Learner Profile attribute 'Principled'.
The representation, intentionally or not, of the ideas, words, or work of another person without proper, clear, and explicit acknowledgement.
Assesses the extent to which the essay shows engagement with the research process. A well-curated bibliography and the effective integration of sources are key evidence for this criterion.
Assesses the formal elements of the essay. A consistent and accurate bibliography, title page, and table of contents are explicitly required for the highest marks (5-6).
A brief reference within your main text that indicates the source of a specific piece of information. For example: (Said, 1978, p. 23).
An alphabetised list of all sources cited in your essay, formatted according to a specific academic style. It appears at the very end of the EE, after the conclusion.
Restating another person's idea in your own words and sentence structure. This requires a citation. Simply changing a few words ('patchwriting') is considered plagiarism.
Using someone else's exact words. Must be enclosed in quotation marks and followed by an in-text citation that includes a specific page number.

Information that is widely known and undisputed within a field, which does not require a citation (e.g., 'Paris is the capital of France'). When in doubt about whether something is common knowledge, it is always safer to cite it.
Software (e.g., Zotero, Mendeley) that helps you collect, organise, cite, and format your sources. Using one is highly recommended for a project the size of the EE.
Submitting your own work for assessment which has already been submitted for another assessment component or qualification. You cannot reuse large sections of an IA or other coursework in your EE.
A source, such as a book or journal article, that has been peer-reviewed and published by a recognised academic press or journal. These are the most credible sources for an EE.
First-hand evidence or raw data from the time period under study. Examples: novels, poems, diaries, letters, lab results, government statistics, artworks.
A source that interprets, analyses, or comments on primary sources. Examples: literary criticism, historical analysis books, review articles in science journals.

faq.q: Can I use Wikipedia for my Extended Essay?
faq.a: You can use Wikipedia as a starting point to get a general overview of a topic and, more importantly, to scroll to the 'References' section at the bottom to find credible, academic sources. You should not, however, cite Wikipedia itself in your final essay, as it is not considered a stable or scholarly source.

faq.q: What if I can't find the author or publication date for a website?
faq.a: This is a major red flag indicating a lack of credibility. Reputable academic, journalistic, or organisational sources will always have clear authorship and a publication or 'last updated' date. If this information is missing, you should question the source's reliability and find a better one. Your EE should be built on credible evidence.

faq.q: Do I need to cite images, graphs, or data tables I use?
faq.a: Yes, absolutely. Any content that you did not create yourself must be cited. For images, graphs, and tables, you should include a caption directly beneath the item with a brief citation (e.g., 'Figure 1. From Smith, 2019, p. 45') and include the full reference in your main bibliography. Failure to do so is plagiarism.

faq.q: How many sources should my EE have?
faq.a: There is no 'magic number'. Quality is far more important than quantity. The number will vary by subject. A History EE might have 20-30 sources, while a Physics EE based on a single, complex experiment might have 10-15. The key is to have engaged with enough high-quality, relevant sources to investigate your research question thoroughly and persuasively. Your supervisor can provide guidance for your specific subject.

sections.content: Welcome to this essential lesson on academic honesty and citation. In the context of the Extended Essay, these are not merely rules to be followed to avoid punishment. Instead, they are the fundamental practices of a principled and inquiring scholar. Mastering citation is your opportunity to demonstrate the rigour of your research, the credibility of your argument, and your readiness for university-level academic work. This lesson will show you how impeccable citation is a direct pathway to higher marks in multiple EE assessment criteria.
Beyond Rules: The Positive Power of Citation

Many students view citation as a chore, a final hurdle before submission. A high-scoring EE student, however, understands that citation is a powerful tool used throughout the research process. It is the mechanism by which you place your own research into conversation with the work of experts who have come before you. Every citation is a deliberate choice that strengthens your essay.

items: Builds Credibility: By citing authoritative sources, you show the examiner that your arguments are not based on opinion but are grounded in established knowledge.

Supports Argument (Criterion B): Your claims are only as strong as the evidence you provide. Citations are the evidence. They allow you to say, 'Don't just take my word for it; here is the data/expert opinion that proves it.'

Demonstrates Engagement (Criterion C): Your bibliography and in-text citations are the primary evidence for your engagement with the research process. A diverse and well-utilised set of sources shows the examiner the depth of your investigation.
Enables Verification: Proper citation allows your supervisor and the examiner to locate your sources, verifying your interpretation and appreciating the intellectual work you have done.

sections.content: The Mechanics: Quoting, Paraphrasing, and Summarising

Effectively integrating sources into your writing is an art. You must choose the right technique for the right situation. Simply dropping in quotes is not enough; you must weave the source material into the fabric of your own argument.

items: Quoting: Use sparingly for impact. Reserve direct quotes for moments when the author's original phrasing is particularly eloquent, powerful, or is the very object of your analysis (e.g., analysing a line from a poem). Always introduce, integrate, and analyse the quote. Never leave a quote to speak for itself.

Paraphrasing: This will be your most-used technique. It involves taking a specific idea from a source and restating it entirely in your own words and sentence structure. This shows the examiner that you have understood the idea, not just copied it. It still requires a citation.

Summarising: This involves condensing the main argument of a longer piece of text (e.g., an entire chapter or article) into a sentence or two. It is useful for providing context or indicating a general scholarly consensus before you introduce your own nuanced argument.

sections.question: You are writing a Psychology EE on the reliability of eyewitness testimony, using the work of Elizabeth Loftus. How would you integrate a key finding from her 1974 study on car crashes using a quote and a paraphrase?
sections.solution:
Original Source Idea: In a classic study, Loftus and Palmer (1974) showed participants a film of a car accident. The verb used to ask about the cars' speed—'smashed', 'collided', 'hit', 'contacted'—systematically affected the participants' speed estimates and their memory of seeing broken glass.

**Ineffective Integration (and poor paraphrasing):**
Loftus and Palmer (1974) did a study on memory. They showed people a film of a car crash and asked them how fast the cars were going when they smashed, collided, or hit each other. They found that the words used changed how fast people thought the cars were going. This shows memory can be changed.

**Effective Integration (Criterion B - Argument & Evidence):**

**Using a Paraphrase:**
The constructive nature of memory is powerfully demonstrated by the work of Loftus and Palmer. Their 1974 study revealed that memory is not a passive recording but an active reconstruction influenced by post-event information. By asking participants to estimate the speed of vehicles after a crash, they found that the simple substitution of a verb, such as 'smashed' instead of 'contacted', could significantly alter speed estimates and even implant false memories of non-existent broken glass (Loftus & Palmer, 1974). This suggests that the testimony of an eyewitness, however confident, may be contaminated by the very language used during questioning.

**Using a Quote:**
Further supporting this concept, Loftus and Palmer argue that two different kinds of information contribute to memory. The first is the information from the perception of the original event, and the second is 'external information supplied after the fact' (1974, p. 586). When these two sources are integrated, a 'memory' is formed that can be distorted and unreliable. This distinction is crucial for understanding how leading questions from law enforcement could inadvertently corrupt an eyewitness's recollection.

**Examiner's View:** The effective examples demonstrate a clear understanding of the source and use it to build a sophisticated argument. The paraphrase is concise and accurate, while the quote is used to introduce a specific theoretical concept from the original authors. This is excellent evidence for a well-supported argument (Criterion B).

sections.content: Mastering a Citation Style for Criterion D

The IB does not mandate a single citation style, but it demands absolute consistency in the one you choose. Your school or supervisor will recommend a style appropriate for your subject (e.g., MLA for English, APA for Psychology, Chicago for History). Mastering the small details of your chosen style is a direct requirement for achieving the top band of Criterion D (Presentation).

sections.question: A student has used a book and a website in their Biology EE. Show the correct APA 7th Edition format for the bibliography and explain how this addresses Criterion D.
sections.solution:
Source 1 (Book): *The Selfish Gene* by Richard Dawkins, 4th edition, published in 2016 by Oxford University Press.
Source 2 (Website): An article titled 'Eusociality in Hymenoptera' on the website of the Nature Education Knowledge Project, written by Jennifer L. Fewell in 2010.

**Incorrect/Inconsistent Bibliography:**
- Richard Dawkins, The Selfish Gene, Oxford, 2016
- https://www.nature.com/scitable/knowledge/library/eusociality-in-hymenoptera-13235412/

**Correct APA 7th Bibliography Entry (Criterion D - Excellent):**

References

Dawkins, R. (2016). *The selfish gene* (4th ed.). Oxford University Press.

Fewell, J. L. (2010). Eusociality in Hymenoptera. *Nature Education Knowledge Project, 3*(10), 7.

**Criterion D Analysis:** An examiner sees the 'Incorrect' version and immediately caps the Criterion D score at a low level. It shows a lack of care and academic rigour. The 'Correct' version, however, signals excellence. It is alphabetised. It uses a hanging indent. It correctly formats author names, capitalisation of titles (*sentence case* for articles, *title case* for books), italics, and edition information. It provides a stable reference for the web source, not just a raw URL. This meticulous attention to the formal requirements of academic presentation is precisely what is needed to score 5-6 marks in Criterion D. It is a non-negotiable element of a top-tier essay.

sections.content: The Bibliography: Your Research Story for Criterion C

Your bibliography is more than a list; it is a narrative of your research journey. An examiner will often look at your bibliography before reading the essay to get an initial impression of the quality of your research. A bibliography filled with only generic websites or outdated textbooks suggests a superficial investigation. A bibliography that shows a range of scholarly books, peer-reviewed journal articles, and relevant primary sources tells the examiner that you have engaged deeply with your topic.

items: Breadth and Depth: Have you consulted a variety of source types? Have you engaged with the foundational texts in your field as well as more recent scholarship?
Relevance: Do the sources listed directly support the investigation of your specific research question?
Scholarly Quality: Are your sources from reputable academic publishers and peer-reviewed journals? This demonstrates that you are participating in a genuine academic conversation.
Active Use: A long bibliography is useless if the sources are not actively integrated into your essay's argument. Criterion C rewards the *use* of sources, not just their collection.

sections.content: Do not 'pad' your bibliography with sources you have not read or cited. Examiners are experts in their fields and can easily spot a mismatch between the sources listed and the arguments presented in the essay. This can be viewed as academic dishonesty. A well-used list of 15 high-quality sources is far more impressive than a padded list of 40 irrelevant ones.

sections.label: Test Your Knowledge on Academic Honesty
